questions.question: What are the six core principles of trauma-informed music and movement practice?

options.text: Speed, intensity, performance, competition, force, perfection
Safety, choice/control, pacing, grounding, boundaries, professional support when needed
Loud music, eyes closed, intense movement, pushing through discomfort, no breaks, solo practice only
Trauma-informed principles don't apply to music and movement

questions.explanation: Trauma-informed principles prioritize: (1) Safety (physical/emotional), (2) Choice and control (full autonomy over practice), (3) Pacing (go slow, titrate intensity), (4) Grounding (anchor in present moment), (5) Boundaries (respect limits, permission to stop), (6) Professional support (know when therapy is needed). These principles rebuild safety and agency after trauma, which often involves experiences of powerlessness and unsafety.

questions.question: You're practicing expressive dance and suddenly feel disconnected from your body (dissociation). What's the trauma-informed response?

options.text: Push through and keep dancing to 'work through' the dissociation
Stop dancing, open eyes if closed, press feet into floor, name 5 objects you can see, take slow breaths, only resume if grounded
Lie down and close your eyes to relax
Immediately do high-intensity jumping jacks to wake yourself up

questions.explanation: ✓ Correct. When dissociation occurs, the trauma-informed response is: (1) stop the activating practice, (2) open eyes (present-moment orientation), (3) ground physically (feet on floor), (4) engage external senses (name 5 objects—interrupts internal dissociative state), (5) regulate breath (slow breathing). Return to window of tolerance before deciding whether to continue. Pushing through deepens dissociation. Lying down/eyes closed can worsen it. Sudden intensity is jarring.

questions.question: Why should trauma survivors often keep eyes open and lights on during movement practices?

options.text: To see better
Eyes-closed practices can intensify dissociation or trigger flashbacks. Eyes open help you stay oriented to the safe present environment, reducing risk of losing contact with reality
Eyes closed is always safer for trauma survivors
Eye position doesn't matter for trauma

questions.explanation: For trauma survivors, eyes-closed practices can intensify dissociation (feeling unreal, disconnected) or trigger flashbacks (vivid re-experiencing of trauma). Eyes open helps you stay connected to your safe present environment (seeing the room, knowing where you are, sensing current reality). This is especially important for people with PTSD or complex trauma. Once safety is established, some survivors can gradually try eyes-closed practices, but eyes open is safer initially.

questions.question: What is the evidence grade for trauma-focused therapies (EMDR, somatic experiencing, trauma-focused DMT) delivered by trained clinicians?

options.text: LIMITED—no meaningful research
MODERATE-STRONG—trauma-focused therapies by trained clinicians show moderate-to-strong evidence for PTSD and complex trauma
EMERGING—only anecdotal evidence
NEGATIVE—trauma therapies worsen outcomes

questions.explanation: Trauma-focused therapies delivered by trained clinicians (EMDR, prolonged exposure, CPT, somatic experiencing, trauma-focused DMT/music therapy) have MODERATE-STRONG evidence for PTSD and complex trauma. These are phase-based approaches: (1) safety/stabilization, (2) trauma processing, (3) integration. The evidence supports professional trauma therapy—self-guided trauma processing is not safe or effective. Self-guided music/movement can support general regulation but not trauma processing.

questions.question: When should self-guided music/movement practices be avoided in favor of professional trauma therapy?

options.text: When you want general stress relief
When you have PTSD, complex trauma, flashbacks, severe dissociation, suicidal thoughts, or when practices consistently trigger overwhelming distress
When you prefer one music genre over another
Never—self-guided work is always sufficient

questions.explanation: Self-guided music/movement is appropriate for general wellness and mild regulation. Seek professional trauma therapy for: PTSD, complex trauma (childhood abuse, chronic trauma), flashbacks, severe dissociation, suicidal thoughts, or when practices consistently trigger overwhelming distress. Trauma *processing* (working through traumatic memories) requires containment, pacing, and expertise that self-guided work cannot provide. Know the difference: regulation (self-guided appropriate) vs. processing (professional support required).

title: Trauma-Informed Music and Movement Practice - Quiz
